stories.title: US gov't revokes controversial AI hardware export rule that would mandate investments from foreign companies — new export rules are still in the works, though

Professors Say AI Is Destroying Their Students’ Ability to Think
75% of resumes never reach a human: the new rules of job searching in the AI era

Andrew Yang says we should stop taxing workers — and start taxing AI

synthesis:
**What This Means for Human Dignity & Labor**

Governments and institutions are abandoning the infrastructure that previously translated human effort into verifiable credentials and economic protection, leaving workers exposed to machines that can perform the tasks those credentials were meant to prove. The U.S. revoked its hardware export rule precisely because direct coercion fails when the underlying technology distributes too widely—meanwhile China's OpenClaw ban and resume-screening AI demonstrate that gatekeeping happens downstream, after displacement has already occurred. Schools like Nanyang Business School are explicitly shifting away from "knowledge transmission" toward soft skills because they've conceded that AI has already broken the link between study and mastery, while simultaneously, 75% of resumes never reach humans because employers optimize for machine parsing rather than human judgment.

This news **accelerates** the structural collapse of performance-based identity into pure competitive desperation, with no institution yet offering an alternative basis for dignity or belonging. Watch Andrew Yang's AI tax proposal—specifically whether it gains traction in the 2024 U.S. policy cycle, because it's the only concrete mechanism being debated that would decouple survival from labor output by redirecting the economic incentives that currently make automation cheaper than employment.
